region_of_origin: India (Tamil Nadu / South India); popular across Indian diaspora in Singapore
food_subgroup: Battered and deep-fried vegetable snack / starter

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Gobi 65. Mapped to closest parent 'deep-fried food product' (FOODON_03301710).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for battered deep-fried spiced cauliflower. A0CKP (Cauliflower, cooked) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Gobi 65 or equivalent battered fried cauliflower dish.

preparation_method: tossed with curry leaves and garlic
allergens: gluten (wheat flour may be present in batter)
possible cross-contamination with nuts in restaurant settings

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Gobi 65 is approximately 150 g, presented as a starter or side dish of crispy battered cauliflower florets garnished with curry leaves and sliced chillies.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Cauliflower preparations and deep-fried snack analogues
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact ICMR-NIN entry for Gobi 65 exists. Values are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN data for raw cauliflower (per 100 g edible portion) with ICMR-NIN data for deep-fried besan-battered vegetable snacks (pakora/bhajia category), adjusted for typical batter absorption during deep frying (~25–30% fat uptake). USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) cauliflower and chickpea flour entries were used as cross-reference for micronutrient values. The composite estimate reflects a battered, deep-fried cauliflower product with spice tempering.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — battered/fried legume-flour coated vegetable analogues; chickpea flour-based snack foods
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Gobi 65. GI estimated at 52 based on analogous chickpea flour (besan) battered deep-fried snack foods reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Chickpea flour has a GI of approximately 39–55 (low range); the batter coating and deep-frying process moderately raises the effective GI of the composite dish. A conservative mid-range estimate of 52 is used, placing the dish in the low GI category (≤55). Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (52 × 24.5 × 1.5) / 100 = 19.1 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g) from spice blends, salt in batter, and tempering
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Gobi 65 to small portions (≤100 g) and request reduced-salt preparation, as a 150 g serving contributes approximately 630 mg sodium — about 27% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended by clinical guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (GL ~19 per serving) from chickpea-flour batter and carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Gobi 65 has a low GI (~52) due to the chickpea flour batter, but the glycemic load per standard 150 g serving is approximately 19 g, which is moderate; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume it in controlled portions alongside low-GI accompaniments and monitor postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat content (~14.8 g per 100 g) from deep frying, with predominantly unsaturated fats but elevated caloric density
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Although saturated fat is relatively low (~1.6 g per 100 g) and cholesterol is negligible, the high total fat and caloric density from deep frying make Gobi 65 a food to consume occasionally and in small portions for individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Gobi 65 is a popular South Indian deep-fried cauliflower dish, widely served as a starter or snack across Indian restaurants in Singapore and throughout the Indian diaspora. Cauliflower florets are coated in a spiced batter made primarily from chickpea flour (besan) and rice flour, seasoned with red chilli powder, turmeric, garam masala, ginger, and garlic, then deep-fried until golden and crispy. The fried florets are finished with a hot tempering of curry leaves, dried red chillies, and garlic in oil, giving the dish its characteristic aroma and flavour. The name '65' is associated with the Chicken 65 dish originating in Tamil Nadu, India, and the vegetarian cauliflower version follows the same preparation philosophy. The texture is crispy on the outside with a tender cauliflower interior. The flavour profile is intensely spiced, slightly tangy, and aromatic. Nutritionally, Gobi 65 is a moderate-calorie dish (~245 kcal per 100 g) with significant fat from deep frying (~14.8 g per 100 g), moderate carbohydrate from the batter (~24.5 g per 100 g), and modest protein (~5.2 g per 100 g). The chickpea flour batter contributes dietary fibre (~3.2 g per 100 g) and gives the dish a low glycemic index (~52). Sodium is elevated (~420 mg per 100 g) due to salt and spice blends. The dish is vegetarian, vegan, and halal-friendly. Clinically, it is moderate concern for hypertension due to sodium, and moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes and cardiovascular disease due to glycemic load and fat content from frying.
common_user_queries: What is the GI of Gobi 65?
What is the difference between Gobi 65 and Chicken 65?
What flour is used in Gobi 65 batter?

confidence_reason: No exact match for Gobi 65 exists in ICMR-NIN published tables. Nutrient values are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN data for raw cauliflower and deep-fried besan-battered snack analogues (pakora/bhajia), cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy. GI is an analogous estimate from chickpea flour-based fried snack data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the estimation methodology is systematic, the analogue foods are well-characterised, and the composite values are internally consistent.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated composites; no single ICMR-NIN or USDA entry covers Gobi 65 exactly. Values should be verified against laboratory analysis if used for clinical dietary planning. 2. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): cauliflower and chickpea flour contain no cholesterol; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy for plant-based ingredients. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; vitamin B12 is naturally zero. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): no vitamin D-containing ingredients are present; confirmed zero for cauliflower and chickpea flour per USDA SR Legacy. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from ICMR-NIN cauliflower and USDA SR Legacy chickpea flour data; these are not -1 sentinel values but estimated composites. 6. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate; gi_category is low (≤55 per Veritas threshold rules). 7. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (52 × 24.5 × 1.5) / 100 ≈ 19.1 g. 8. The dish is served at Mr Tandoor - Boat Quay on field survey data at SGD 15.90. 9. Allergen note: batter may contain wheat flour in some restaurant preparations; gluten-free status cannot be guaranteed without restaurant confirmation.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built full record from scratch. Estimated nutrients from ICMR-NIN cauliflower and deep-fried besan snack data cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous chickpea flour fried snack data. Multilingual names provided for Chinese (simplified/traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi per Indian/Singaporean cuisine rules. All zero values (cholesterol, vitamin B12, vitamin D) documented with 'confirmed zero' justification in notes. All ontology match_type values restricted to allowed set. aliases_by_language and flat aliases verified for consistency. All hard gates and Q1-Q8 criteria satisfied in first iteration.
